title: Bezos's 'Project Prometheus' Nears $10B Round at $38B Valuation
subtitle: BlackRock and JPMorgan are leading a round for the Bezos-led AI lab building models that understand the physical world — his first operational role since leaving Amazon.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Jeff Bezos is close to closing a $10 billion funding round for Project Prometheus, the AI lab he co-founded with former Google DeepMind scientist Vikram Bajaj, the Financial Times reported on April 21. BlackRock and JPMorgan are anchoring the round at a $38 billion valuation. Once complete, Prometheus will have raised more than $16 billion in total, including the $6.2 billion it took in at its November 2025 launch. The company is headquartered in San Francisco with offices in London and Zurich, and Bezos is co-CEO — his first operational role since stepping down from Amazon in 2021.

Prometheus is focused on what the industry calls 'physical AI' or 'world models': systems trained to predict how physical environments behave under the laws of physics, rather than next-token prediction over text. The stated applications are industrial — engineering simulation, manufacturing, materials design — territory that sits well outside the chatbot and coding-assistant markets where most frontier labs compete. Bezos is reportedly also raising up to $100 billion for a separate holding company that would acquire industrial businesses being disrupted by AI, a sign that the Prometheus bet is as much about vertical integration into the real economy as it is about model capability.

The round lands in a market that has normalized nine-and-ten-figure rounds for frontier AI: OpenAI's $122B raise earlier this month, Anthropic's $19B annualized revenue, and Cursor's $2B round at $50B+ all arrived within weeks of each other. What sets Prometheus apart is investor composition — BlackRock and JPMorgan are late-stage growth investors, not venture funds, which signals that physical AI is being priced as infrastructure rather than as speculative research. It also signals that the traditional venture stack is no longer large enough to fund frontier AI companies at the valuations they now command.

For learners, the interesting shift here is the 'physical AI' framing. Language models learned from text; the next wave is learning from simulation and the physical world — robotics, materials, protein structure, fluid dynamics. Students considering an AI specialization should notice that the field is broadening well beyond LLMs, and that physics, mechanical engineering, and classical simulation skills are becoming valuable complements to machine learning training rather than alternatives to it.
